TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 1 Practicum Prospectus: Tele Critical Care Implementation and Education Project Jeffrey Dover University of San Francisco TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT Project Charter...................................................................................... 51 TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 4 Abstract Problem: The intensive care unit has the highest rate of mortality and patient care costs in the hospital. There are significant challenges in improving care and controlling costs. One solution adopted by many healthcare organizations is intensive care telemedicine. Commonly known as eICUs or Tele Critical Care (TCC). These specialty units provide access to intensivists and nurses 24-hours a day and has shown success in reducing mortality and length of stay while providing cost savings. Challenges exist in introducing telemedicine to a new setting. Barriers identified are high initial cost of equipment, competing priorities for resources, and nurse acceptance. The purpose of this paper is to focus on improving RN acceptance and perceived usefulness of telemedicine technology by providing staff education through a live class to introduce and familiarize staff with the benefits and rationale for implementing TCC in the target unit. Context: An 8-bed ICU located in a 50-bed medical center was selected as the target unit for this project. The study population consists of ICU nurses regularly staffed to the unit who will have regular interactions with TCC. Intervention: A pre and postsurvey using a validated tool, the TCC Implementation Survey, was envisioned for use in this project. These data will inform the direction and content of the educational component. Additional data from ICU quality reports could also guide development of the presentations. The postsurvey would be administered following the class. The goal of this project is to increase staff acceptance and perceived usefulness by 15% above presurvey levels with a 75% attendance rate in class and 80% response rate to the survey. It was intended for the project to go forward contemporaneously with TCC implementation. Due to impacts of the COVID-19, TCC implementation was halted and the TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 5 initial plan could not go forward. Also, discussions with union-represented employees did not occur, preventing their participation as a survey group. As a substitute, a small convenience sample of non-represented employees was selected; however, due to IRB concerns for anonymity, the original survey could not be used with this group. A modified qualitative survey was selected and administered to this cohort. The 25-question survey focused on areas of quality, safety, communication, availability of ICU consultation, and clinical decision making. The small sample size of nine participants limited making statistically significant conclusions regarding the results, but the impressions given by the responses give insight into staff acceptance and opportunities to improve care. Results: The results were graded on a scale of 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). Of note, the question, “I think new technology would diminish independence in my practice,” was found to have the lowest score of 2.4, indicating the staff seem receptive to new technology. In the sections, “Safety climate in your ICU” and “Quality outcomes in your ICU,” the lowest scores were regarding RNs’ (3.2) and doctors’ (3.1.) rounding on the unit, showing a possible need for improvement. Clinical decision-making at night found a positive association regarding the management of antibiotics and pain and sedative medication, with scores of 4.2 in each category. Whereas, the decision to intubate had the lowest mark in this section (3.6). An indication that decision-making in this area may need to be supported. Conclusions: The initial planning and tools are readily available to proceed when the TCC program resumes. Utilizing the modified survey allowed some data gathering and provided insights into the ICU staff’s perceived needs and perceptions of technology. Going forward, the design of this project could be used to support new-hire onboarding and provide a refresher to staff during annual skill events. TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 6 Section II: Introduction Ever since the publication of To Err is Human: Building a Safer Health System (Institute of Medicine, 2000) brought to light that up 98,000 people a year die as a result of avoidable harm in hospitals and healthcare settings in the United States, the manner in which healthcare identifies and approaches the issue of patient safety and avoidable harm has fundamentally shifted. Nowhere in healthcare is the issue of patient mortality more acute than in the intensive care unit (ICU). Having the highest mortality rate in the hospital (10%-20%), as well the highest costs in healthcare (4.1% of all spending or an estimated $107 billion annually), there is a clear need to improve care and reduce avoidable harm from both a humanistic and financial perspective. Contemporaneously, the number and acuity of critically ill patients has been increasing, while the supply of critical care physicians available to care for this vulnerable population has been decreasing (Network for Excellence in Health Innovation [NEHI], 2011). The issue of job attrition is not a physician-only problem. As our population ages, so do many of the nation’s experienced critical care nurses. It is projected that as many as one million of the nation’s current 3.8 million registered nurses (RNs) will retire between 2020 and 2030, and with them, their years of experience will disappear from practice (Buerhaus et al., 2017). Problem Description The challenge is how does the healthcare system address the increasing need for more and better care, while at the same time dealing with a shrinking workforce. A solution adopted by some healthcare organizations is the use of telemedicine in the ICU setting. Developed as a method of bringing critical care expertise to the bedside, ICU telemedicine units, also known as electronic ICUs (eICUs) or tele critical care (TCC), are staffed with board-certified critical care TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 7 physicians and experienced critical care nurses who are able to monitor patients from remote locations. As part of the ICU team, these professionals are able to evaluate labs and studies, place orders, and evaluate compliance with evidence-based practice standards in the ICU (Canfield & Galvin, 2018). This technology has had demonstrated success. In a rapid demonstration project at the University of Massachusetts Memorial Medical Center (UMMMC) and two affiliated community hospitals, there was shown to be a decrease in mortality of 20% at UMMMC and 36% decrease in the community sites, while at the same time, patient acuity increased, as determined by APACHE III score. In addition, ICU length of stay decreased by 30% overall (NEHI, 2011, p. 3). Seeing the benefits of introducing this technology, a large healthcare system in Northern California has undertaken a project to implement TCC in all 21 medical centers in the region. With the potential benefits, barriers still exist to successful implementation of tele-ICU technology. Those barriers identified in the literature range from initial high capital cost, competing priorities for information technology project financing, and physician resistance (NEHI, 2011). In addition to these significant challenges is the issue of ICU nurse acceptance. As the end-user of this technology, the bedside nurse plays a critical role in the success of any tele-ICU program. Concerns identified by nurses are of perceived intrusiveness of tele-ICU monitoring, the sense that they are being spied upon, and the possible impact of the tele-ICU on current workflows (Canfield & Galvin, 2018). The system setting targeted by this project is an 8-bed ICU located in a small urban medical center. This 50-bed facility is in an inner-city community, which serves a mixed population of both health plan members and non-members. This ICU has intensivist coverage TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 8 from 8:00 a.m. to 8:00 p.m. daily, with on-call coverage after hours, and a high rate of bed utilization (approximately 80% occupancy). Available Knowledge PICOT Question (P) In critical care nurses, (I) how does an educational intervention effect perception of usefulness and ease of use of tele critical care technology (C), compared to pre-implementation attitudes? Available Evidence An electronic search process was undertaken to determine the current state of evidence regarding ICU telemedicine. Searches were conducted via CINAHL and PUBMED databases using the search terms eICU and ICU telemedicine. This search yielded 54 results, with 14 related to the subject. Of those, six were deemed relevant to the project goals, with three directly examining the issue of telemedicine presence and three examining staff attitudes toward the introduction of new technologies in the healthcare setting. Two industry reports supporting tele-ICU were also included for review, as they provided a general context and some evidence supporting the use of this technology. An evaluation table of the accepted materials was completed and is available in Appendix A. Two reports produced by NEHI show support for the use and expansion of ICU telemedicine. In 2011, NEHI asserted that the use of tele-ICU produced significant cost savings, as well as a demonstrable reduction in mortality (as much as 40%) and ICU length of stay (25%). The 2013 NEHI report advocated expanding coverage of tele-ICU to rural and critical access hospitals, rotating clinicians through hospital and non-clinical settings to enhance communication and build relationships with bedside staff. TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 9 In a mixed-methods study examining the introduction of a new acute stroke telemedicine service, Bagot et al. (2020) performed a pre- and post-implementation study with a limited number of semi-structured interviews. Their findings supported the conclusion that nurses play an influential role in implementing and sustaining a complex new system, such as acute telemedicine, and that attitudes toward a new technology can shift after implementation. It was also noted that tailored pre-implementation strategies, such as clinical support and education, were important factors in successful implementation (Bagot et al., 2020). In a review of the history of ICU telemedicine and its acceptance by bedside nurses, Canfield and Galvin (2018) also advocated for the use of tele-ICU technology, while also noting barriers to acceptance, such as concerns for privacy and perceived intrusiveness. Kowitlawakul (2011) surveyed a pool of 117 nurses’ intention to use eICU technology and was able to validate the use of the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) as an effective tool in explaining nurses’ decision to accept a new technology and their intention to use eICU technology. In addition, Kowitlawakul noted that to support autonomy and competency, educational opportunities should be presented to allow adaptation to new technologies. Three articles were included to demonstrate the validity of the TAM as a tool to gauge healthcare workers’ intention to use, their perceived ease of use, and the perceived usefulness of a given technology. These measures are widely accepted as determinants to overall acceptance of a given technology. Although not specific to ICU telemedicine, these studies can serve to show barriers and possible approaches to influence behavior of staff to embrace new technologies. Mussa et al.’s (2019) study of a small cohort of respiratory therapists used an educational intervention involving lecture and small group interactions. A pre- and post-survey based on TAM showed an improvement on staff knowledge and intention to use on newly introduced TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 10 medical early warning score. Schnall and Bakken (2011) examined the applicability of TAM on HIV case managers’ intention to use an electronic charting system. In a study of 94 case managers, the authors concluded that the TAM constructs were valid and could be applied to this group of healthcare workers (Schnall & Bakken, 2011). Finally, Lin (2017) examined nurses’ satisfaction using a nursing information system to improve efficiency and quality of care. Utilizing surveys based on the TAM and the Information Systems Success Model, Lin surveyed a group of 531 RNs. The author concluded that nurses’ perception of technology, perceived usefulness, and perceived ease of use were all critical determinants to their satisfaction using the system and should guide leaders in the development and adoption of new technologies. Available evidence shows that tele-ICU technology can be used to improve the quality of care and reduce costs in the intensive care setting. In addition, staff attitudes and perceptions of new technologies introduced into their care setting can influence the usefulness and ultimate success of a tele-ICU system. The TAM has been shown to be a valid tool for assessing staff perceptions of new technologies. Finally, the interventions of providing staff education, interactive communication, and relationship building between the ICU nursing staff, leadership, and the tele-ICU team has been identified as effective and supportive to successful implementation of telemedicine modalities. Rationale Two models were chosen to guide the direction of this project. The first is the TAM. The TAM was originally developed by Fred Davis and Richard Bagozzi in the 1980s out of concern that workers were not using technology available to them. By examining the factors that influence individual’s acceptance of and intention to use a technology, an organization can design systems to better accommodate the end-users (Holden & Karsh, 2010). Although not a TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 11 nursing theory, it has been modified for use in healthcare and focuses on the perceived ease of use, intention to use, and perceived usefulness as key measures to determine intention to use of the target audience (Kowitlawakul, 2011). Caring science is a theory of nursing care developed by Jean Watson in 1979. The tenants are based on providing nursing care focused on compassion, loving-kindness, and respect for the individual (Watson, 2018). This framework was adopted by the healthcare system as a model for nursing care in 2010 to provide more patient- and family-centric care in its medical centers. This is achieved by supporting principles of promoting safety, comfort, engagement, nurturing, connectedness, and empowerment (Foss Durant et al., 2015). At first glance, it may be difficult to reconcile the differing perspectives embodied by TAM and caring science, but several of Watson’s caritas processes, including authentic presence, developing trusting caring relationships, allowing expression of positive and negative feelings, and creative problem solving, are all principles that could support successful implementation of TCC. It is hoped that integrating the principles of caring science into this project will support the essential elements of collaboration and communication necessary for successful implementation of TCC. Specific Project Aim The specific aim of this project is to involve the staff nurses in an educational intervention to improve their perceptions of TCC’s usefulness and ease of use by 15% from pre-survey values. TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 12 Section III: Methods Context This quality improvement project will take place in an 8-bed medical ICU. This unit serves a mixed population of roughly 50% health plan members and 50% non-members. Age breakdown is estimated to be 75% greater than 70 years old. Major diagnoses in this population include sepsis, congestive heart failure, respiratory failure, and altered mentation, including alcohol withdrawal disorder and illicit drug intoxication and withdrawal. RN staffing is dependent on census and anticipated needs. The staffing mix includes a break and relief nurse and nurses staffed at a 2:1 ratio, with a typical maximum of five RNs. This does not account for patients requiring care at an enhanced ratio, which can increase total unit staffing to six or seven RNs. Intensivist coverage consists of two physicians with overlapping schedules providing in-house coverage from 8:00 a.m. to 8:00 p.m., with a physician designated on call for after hour needs. In-house coverage after hours is provided by the onsite hospital-based specialist. Other team members include respiratory therapists and a unit clerk. An analysis of the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats (SWOT) identified for the implementation of TCC was performed (see Appendix B). Strengths identified were 24-hour intensivist availability and improved compliance with evidence-based practice guidelines, such as electrolyte replacement, ventilator optimization, and adherence to physician orders for titration of drips. Weaknesses noted were staff and physician resistance to new technology and changed workflow and communication issues between managing physician services and TCC physicians. Opportunities anticipated would be decreases in ICU mortality, ICU length of stay, and physician and nurse workload. Threats may include labor/management conflict regarding TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 13 change in workflows, delayed implementation due to budgetary issues, and unanticipated impacts from the evolving COVID-19 situation. Financial benefits in terms of return on investment can be anticipated beyond the initial capital cost of installation and upgrading of hardware in the ICU. The recovery of these initial funds could potentially be regained in as little as a year following implementation, with savings from $2,600 to as much as $10,000 per admission, per data derived from similar implementations (NEHI, 2011). Intervention The design of this quality improvement project will center upon a pre- and post-test survey derived with permission from Kowitlawakul (see Appendix C). The eICU acceptance survey will be administered anonymously to the ICU RNs in a paper and pencil format. Pre-implementation data will guide the formation of an educational component that will be composed of live classes and staff interactions. The content of these classes will include an understanding of the purpose of TCC, including its structure, benefits, and technology involved, and introduction to the offsite team and their abilities to support the inpatient unit. Additional interactions are planned to allow expression of concerns and to ask questions regarding implementation. The format of the staff training was designed to promote communication between the implementation team and the ICU staff who will be most affected by TCC. Fostering interactions and two-way communication between the implementation team and ICU staff through in-person classes is intended to support the caring science processes of authentic presence, building trusting relationships, and sharing of both positive and negative feelings. An additional component could also involve site visits to the TCC hub to promote team TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 14 communication between bedside and TCC staff. Following the educational component, a post-survey will be administered to determine what change to nurses’ perceived usefulness and attitudes toward TCC may have occurred due to the educational intervention. With the stated interventions in place and ready, implementation of TCC at the target site has been delayed due to the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. Currently, operations at the TCC have ceased, as the intensivist group staffing TCC have been re-tasked to address anticipated surges and other priorities in the clinical setting. In addition, the leadership group at the target facility has focused their energies into addressing the pandemic-related effects, which has severely limited attention to other initiatives such as this project. As a consequence, necessary negotiations with union-represented nurses have not occurred as yet, which prevents implementation of TCC and any related project such as this. To allow for some data collection related to this project, a modified qualitative survey was rapidly adopted and has been administered to members of the management team. These include assistant nurse managers, nurse managers and nursing supervisors who will be involved in TCC operations when the program is implemented. Adopted with permission of the TCC director, the survey comprises 26 questions broken into seven sections that include nighttime availability of critical care expertise, timing of care safety climate, quality of care, communication, the role of technology, and clinical decision-making. These questions were scored on a 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree) scale. Refer to Appendix C for examples of the original survey and the modified survey. Study of the Intervention Specific measures to be examined from this project will be derived from the results of the pre- and post-implementation survey. The survey is broken down into three sections: perceived TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 15 usefulness, nurse’s attitude toward TCC, and demographic data. There is also a box allowing comments in the first two sections. From these data, average scores can be calculated gauging the results of the parameters and conclusions can be theorized. An alternate survey was adopted, as noted in the interventions section, and used a convenience sample of assistant nurse managers, nurse managers, and house supervisors who will be working directly with TCC, with planning to resume the original education and survey to all involved RNs as installation and go-live of TCC nears. Family of Measures The outcome measures to be tracked will be nurses’ perceived usefulness and attitudes toward implementation of TCC. It is planned to have an improvement of 15% following administration of the educational component. Process measures that can be examined following these interventions could be a decrease in ICU length of stay, mortality, and ventilator days. These measures will not be available during this project, as implementation of TCC is delayed due to the current impacts caused by COVID-19. Post-implementation data collected from the Quality Chasm report will be integrated into monthly staff meetings to show areas of success, as well as opportunities for improvement. Balancing measures that could affect the project may be related to overtime and additional hours necessary for staff participation in the educational presentation. An anticipated maximum time of 30 minutes will be assumed for each class session, with additional hours dependent on consultation with ICU leadership and approval. Ethical Concerns Ethical concerns regarding this project are anticipated to be limited. The survey will be administered anonymously to participating staff members, and there will be no privileged health information included in this project, as individual patients will not be included; however, the TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 16 quality data as an aggregate may be used to inform development of the education plan in the future. Staff concerns will be addressed in a manner consistent with the organization’s policy and procedures and in adherence with any collective bargaining agreements with involved staff. Internal Review Board (IRB) approval was not necessary for this project, as it met criteria for a quality improvement project (see Appendix D). The modified survey used was determined not to require IRB approval and was adopted in place of the original survey. TCC IMPLEMENTATION & EDUCATION PROJECT 17 Section IV: Results The original target group for this project was the ICU nurses staffing the target unit. This group consists of 51 RNs employed in a full-time, part-time, or per diem capacity. Owing to limitations on this project, this group was not able to be surveyed at this time. Instead, a convenience sample consisting of assistant nurse mangers, nurse managers, and house supervisors who would have direct involvement with TCC after implementation was utilized for purposes of this survey. Of 14 possible participants from this group, nine responses were obtained, resulting in a response rate of 64%. Five identified as assistant nurse managers, two as nurse managers, and two as house supervisors. Length of time in the role ranged from six months to 15 years for assistant nurse managers, seven months and three years for managers, with house supervisors having three and six years in their roles. Data were collected from the submitted surveys, and an average score for each question was determined by simple calculation of the sum of the score divided by the number of responses. Of note, one respondent did not answer the section on clinical decision-making; this was reflected in the calculations and scoring for that section. Although the limited sample size prevents any ability to draw statistically significant conclusions, it does offer the opportunity to get impressions of the participants’ views of the care environment. Bar graphs were developed using the survey data and are displayed in Appendix E. Each question was scored separately, and a chart was created for each topic area. The first section titled, “Nighttime availability of critical care expertise in your ICU,” consisted of two questions: Question 1. # Improving critical care through telemedicine: a comprehensive analysis of a Tele-ICU project in northern and northeastern regions of Brazil

[*BMC Health Services Research*](/)
**volume 25**, Article number: 809 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1013 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4/metrics)

## Abstract

### Introduction

The Tele-Intensive Care Unit (Tele-ICU) project aimed to standardize and improve the quality of critical care services, particularly in regions where access to specialized care and resources remains limited. We herein provide a detailed overview of the implementation of the Tele-ICU project in Brazil’s northern and northeastern regions, describing its multi-modal approach, which combined telemedicine implementation, educational initiatives, simulation-based training, protocol development, and structured data reporting to enhance critical care delivery.

### Methods

The implementation of the Tele-ICU project, developed in a partnership between the Ministry of Health and Hospital Sírio-Libanês from June 2022 to December 2023, was supported by the Support Program for Institutional Development of the Unified Health System (PROADI-SUS). The intervention strategy included on-site implementation workshops, tele-rounds, and structured educational initiatives. A set of indicators was collected to monitor the implementation process. A mixed-methods approach was employed to analyze quantitative indicators and qualitative feedback from intensive care unit (ICU) teams.

### Results

A total of 5,471 patients were treated across 15 ICUs through 3,971 tele-rounds. During these tele-rounds, 20,168 telehealth visits—bedside discussions focused on individual patients—were conducted, covering 74% of inpatients. Additionally, 30 educational activities were developed, involving 760 health professionals. Participants reported high levels of satisfaction throughout the project.

### Conclusion

This study demonstrated the feasibility of implementing telemedicine in critical care delivery within resource-limited settings. Our findings suggest that the Tele-ICU model can be a scalable solution for enhancing critical care in these environments.

[Peer Review reports](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4/peer-review)

## Introduction

The field of telemedicine has witnessed remarkable advancements in recent years, marking a new era of patient care across various medical domains. Among these groundbreaking applications is the Tele-Intensive Care Unit (Tele-ICU), which aims to standardize and enhance the quality of critical care services, particularly in regions where access to specialized care and resources remains limited [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#ref-CR1 "Khurrum M, Asmar S, Joseph B. Telemedicine in the ICU: innovation in the critical care process. J Intensive Care Med. 2020;36:1377–84.")].

In 2010, Brazil’s Ministry of Health established guidelines (Resolução da Diretoria Colegiada - RDC 07/2010) for the organization and operation of intensive care units (ICUs) [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#ref-CR2 "Sociedade Brasileira de Terapia Intensiva. RESOLUÇÃO-RDC No 7, DE 24 DE FEVEREIRO DE 2010. 2010.")]. These guidelines established minimum requirements for ICU structure, equipment, and staffing, along with detailed directives for patient care, patient safety, and quality assurance. Unfortunately, a substantial number of ICUs in Brazil fail to meet these stipulated minimum standards [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#ref-CR2 "Sociedade Brasileira de Terapia Intensiva. RESOLUÇÃO-RDC No 7, DE 24 DE FEVEREIRO DE 2010. 2010."), [3](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#ref-CR3 "Carvalho AGR, Moraes APP, Carvalho ACP, Silva AAM. Quality assessment of adult intensive care services: application of a tool adjusted to the reality of low-income countries. Rev Brasil Ter Intensiva. 2019;31(2):138–46.")]. Starting in 2020, we initiated a Tele-ICU project targeting ICUs in Brazil’s northern and northeastern regions that were noncompliant with organizational standards established by RDC 07/2010. This project was developed through a partnership between the Ministry of Health and Hospital Sírio-Libanês from June 2022 to December 2023 with support from the Support Program for Institutional Development of the Unified Health System (PROADI-SUS).

We herein present a detailed overview of this Tele-ICU project, including its implementation phases, challenges, and strategies to address them. The Tele-ICU project was motivated by the pressing need to improve critical care services in regions with limited access to specialized care and resources. This initiative comprised several key components, including implementation workshops, tele-rounds, educational initiatives, simulation-based training, protocol development, and structured data reporting. Together, these components aimed to create a more cohesive and effective ICU environment. By scrutinizing the implementation of this project, we seek to contribute to the broader discussion on the role of telemedicine in critical care and its potential for scalability in similar contexts globally.

## Methods

### Study design

This case study presents the implementation of the Tele-ICU project and was approved by the Ethics Committee of Hospital Sírio-Libanês (Number: 68215723.9.0000.546).

### Selection

The list of potential hospitals and ICUs was determined by the Secretary of Health. From this initial list, we applied the inclusion and exclusion criteria outlined in Table [1](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Tab1). Participation was not mandatory, and the selection process considered the motivation of the participating teams, their commitment to the project’s goals, and adherence to its criteria.

### Tele-ICU operational model

The Tele-ICU project implemented a hub-and-spoke model in which a centralized team at Hospital Sírio-Libanês (HSL) provided remote support to multiple ICUs. This model was selected to maximize the reach of specialized intensive care expertise while ensuring operational feasibility in a setting with limited healthcare infrastructure and specialized personnel.

To support this initiative, a team was assembled to provide remote coverage for 160 ICU beds per day, as established by the Brazilian Ministry of Health. The team included: six critical care physicians (intensivists), three critical care nurses, and four critical care physiotherapists.

The tele-ICU service operated multiple teams per shift (three in the morning, and two in the afternoon). Each team was responsible for managing three ICUs and comprised one critical care physician (intensivist), one critical care nurse, and one critical care physiotherapist. The team structure was designed based on the assumption that each unit could effectively manage approximately 10 patients per hour. However, despite this projection, the project was only able to attend to 70% of the estimated patients.

During their shifts, these teams worked exclusively within the Tele-ICU service, conducting structured daily tele-rounds as the primary communication method. This approach enabled early interventions, real-time clinical decision support, and continuous education for participating ICUs. Each tele-round lasted one hour and focused on collaborative case reviews, ensuring structured high-quality clinical discussions.

Regarding patient monitoring, the project adopted an intermittent model in which clinical data were reviewed during scheduled tele-rounds. This strategy allowed for systematic evaluation and intervention while optimizing resource use.

Outside scheduled tele-rounds, the HSL team remained available to address follow-up questions related to previously discussed cases; however, they did not offer on-demand consultations for new cases.

### Virtual platform

The virtual platform used in the Tele-ICU project was not a commercially available solution but rather a custom-developed platform designed specifically to support the project’s objectives (please refer to <https://www.researchsquare.com/article/rs-3536188/v1>). This platform was built to facilitate tele-rounds, real-time communication, and data integration, while also incorporating electronic health record (EHR) functionalities.

The platform was developed with the following key functionalities: secure video conferencing for tele-rounds and inter-consultations; EHR functionality with structured documentation of patient data, tele-round recommendations, clinical decision-making; and data storage and retrieval features to support follow-up. Data retention policies were aligned with institutional regulations and Brazilian data protection laws (Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados – LGPD). The system allowed healthcare professionals to access and update patient records during each tele-round, improving continuity of care.

As the platform underwent continuous development during the project’s operational phase, training sessions were kept concise, typically lasting 30 to 40 min. To ensure alignment with user needs, monthly follow-up meetings were conducted with the project team to validate and refine the platform functionalities and user interface.

### Implementation

In-person visits were carried out at each participating hospital. The preferred composition of the visiting team included a project coordinator, an intensive care nurse, an intensive care physiotherapist, and an information technology specialist. When logistical constraints or scheduling conflicts prevented certain members from traveling, remote participation was used to ensure continuity of support. Each in-person visit lasted eight hours and was conducted in a room or an auditorium using a video projector for the presentation of the project. The activities included an initial assessment of the hospital and ICU, an explanation of the project’s scope, an introduction to the virtual platform, an integration workshop, and an inaugural tele-round.

During the initial assessment of each hospital and its ICU, the project team conducted a comprehensive review of the local infrastructure. This included evaluating the availability and condition of essential equipment—such as multiparameter monitors, mechanical ventilators, and hospital beds—as well as existing resources and support areas for the ICU. This assessment was crucial for understanding the operational dynamics of each unit and enabled the tailored integration of telemedicine services based on the specific needs of each hospital. The presentation of the project scope to the teams covered the planning of activities related to the project, the operation of the tele-rounds, the functioning of the operational data repository system, the monitoring of indicators, and the team’s support throughout the project.

In the integration workshop, the LEGO™ SERIOUS PLAY™ methodology was used to interact with the participants, who were professionals directly involved in patient care in the ICU, such as medical doctors, nurses, nursing technicians, physiotherapists, nutritionists, speech therapists, psychologists, pharmacists, social workers, and health managers [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#ref-CR4 "Kristiansen P, Rasmussen R. Building a better business using the LEGO® SERIOUS PLAY® method. 1st edition. Hoboken: Wiley; 2014.")]. This activity allowed for discussion about communication within the team, highlighting its importance in the pursuit of results.

The inaugural tele-round was the first synchronous interaction between the Hospital Sírio-Libanês (HSL) team and the ICU’s multidisciplinary team. The HSL team operated from the command room located at Hospital Sírio-Libanês in São Paulo. The command room consisted of a booth with the necessary equipment to facilitate the professionals’ activities in this space. The booth was equipped with a computer with peripherals, an HD camera (Webcam C920e, Logitech, Newark, USA), an audio and voice device (Jabra Speak 510, GN Audio A/S, Ballerup, Denmark), and access to the project’s virtual platform. As a technological resource for the participating ICUs, a tablet (Galaxy Tab A8, Samsung, Manaus, Brazil) was provided, in addition to a mounting bracket and mobile connectivity for each participating ICU, consisting of a 4G internet data plan of up to 80 GB to support the remote connections, mitigating the risks of network instability. To avoid potential connectivity issues associated with the 4G chip, wireless routers (Archer C50 V6, with WiFi speeds of 867 Mbps at 5 GHz and 300 Mbps at 2.4 GHz, Tp-Link, Manaus, Brazil) were provided to the ICUs.

Before starting the tele-round, the HSL team provided a demonstration of the virtual platform, covering key functionalities such as user login, patient registration, access to the tele-round room, viewing and printing the list of goals, and filling out the daily census.

### Tele-rounds

Tele-rounds consisted of daily multidisciplinary visits on weekdays, lasting one hour, at previously arranged times. The HSL customer service operators would contact the unit’s responsible personnel via text message thirty minutes before the tele-round to confirm the participation of each ICU’s multidisciplinary team. After confirmation, the HSL team, positioned in the command room, established a connection with the remote ICU team. Both teams were composed, at a minimum, of a physician, a physiotherapist, and a nurse. The maximum tolerance time for the start of the tele-round was defined as 10 min for the connection of the remote ICU teams to the virtual platform. If there was no connection by the time limit, the tele-round was considered canceled. Through the discussion of the cases of patients admitted to the ICU, a joint alignment between the teams on the conduct of treatment was carried out. Depending on availability, there was the possibility of requesting the participation of physicians from other specialties. The operational model of the tele-round is represented in Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Fig1).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12913-025-12894-4/MediaObjects/12913_2025_12894_Fig1_HTML.png)

Operation model of the tele-round

The tele-round tool was a virtual platform that included the following data: daily census, SAPS-3 (Simplified Acute Physiology Score 3) prognostic index, and clinical data of patients, including the main diagnoses and medical history. In addition, during each tele-round, a care goal plan was established for every patient discussed, contemplating at least the following items: sedation target, weaning off vasoactive drugs, adjustment of fluid balance, weaning from mechanical ventilation and oxygen, extubation, mobilization, nutritional support, glycemic control, electrolyte and blood product control, antibiotics, device removal, pending exams and procedures, psychosocial needs, therapeutic status, and clinical discharge.

For each telehealth visit, the camera position on the tablet was directed at the patient to be discussed. It is important to emphasize that the patients’ confidential data, such as clinical information, images, and results of additional exams, were linked to the patient’s medical records and with access permission exclusively for the professionals who provided care for the case. Other tools, such as messaging apps with end-to-end encryption, were used only for disseminating information about the project without sensitive data. The collected data from the patients and clinical decisions were anonymously stored in a secure virtual environment. The primary goal was to conduct tele-rounds for all ICU patients; however, due to time constraints, discussions focused on a selected number of patients based on prioritization criteria. The cases were prioritized in the following order: (a) newly admitted patients, (b) patients with the highest disease severity, and (c) clinically stable patients.

### Operational and clinical monitoring of the Tele-ICU project

To monitor the implementation of the Tele-ICU project, a set of operational and clinical indicators was collected (Table [2](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Tab2)). These included the number of tele-rounds conducted, adherence to tele-round participation, professional satisfaction (measured using the Net Promoter Score - NPS), and the frequency of telehealth visits. Additionally, clinical care indicators, such as ICU length of stay, ICU mortality, use of invasive mechanical ventilation, central line catheter utilization, and intermittent urinary catheter use, were tracked to provide a comprehensive overview of ICU practices.

The data were obtained from the daily census database and entered into the virtual platform by the nurse responsible for the unit. These data were subsequently reviewed by a specialized team responsible for the quality and analysis of the indicators and for the creation of a dashboard, which was shared with the ICU leaders. To promote team engagement, dashboards were also visually displayed within each ICU using printed acrylic panels. Dashboards were generally made available to ICUs approximately six months after implementation. Hospitals that joined the project after this period received access to dashboards in the following month.

### Educational activities

During tele-round sessions, in addition to providing clinical guidance, the HSL team identified the primary needs and specific demands of each remote ICU. Based on these insights, monthly Virtual Learning Sessions (VLS) were conducted to address perceived gaps in knowledge and practice among care teams. These sessions were offered in two formats: (1) virtual meetings and (2) complex case discussions.

The virtual meetings consisted of online presentations on topics related to the care of critically ill patients. These topics were suggested by the participating ICUs through an online survey. Sessions were conducted synchronously in the afternoon, with all participating ICUs, and lasted up to two hours. Participation was mandatory, with the requirement for at least one professional from each institution. The presentations were recorded and made available asynchronously in a virtual repository (YouTube® and Vimeo®).

The complex case discussions were organized into two types: Theoretical Case Discussions (TCD) and Practical Case Discussions (PCD), both designed to address knowledge gaps identified by the HSL team during tele-rounds. In TCD activities, participating ICUs were divided into three groups to improve the discussion. These sessions were conducted synchronously, with each group of ICUs participating in sessions lasting up to two hours. The HSL team presented the selected topic, incorporating updates of current scientific evidence. The teams of the remote ICUs could ask questions during the activity.

The PCD activities were held in the month following the corresponding TCD session and focused on the practical application of the previously discussed topic. During the PCD, a clinical case from a remote ICU was presented by its multidisciplinary team during the time reserved for the tele-round, with a maximum duration of one hour. Participation in both TCD and PCD sessions was mandatory, with each institution required to involve its multidisciplinary team.

All of the VLS presentations, except for the PCD, were constructed and shared among the HSL team professionals, according to their expertise in the subject. The teams from the remote ICUs could participate in all presentations using the provided tablet or their own devices.

In addition to these educational activities, support materials were provided in the form of simulation videos and educational cards. The simulation video material covered the main scales for assessing the critical patient (Visual Analog Scale, Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale, ICU Mobility Scale, and Confusion Assessment Method for the Intensive Care Unit). In addition to the scales, videos related to mobilization and positioning in bed, administration of high-vigilance medications, and conducting multidisciplinary rounds were recorded. To create realistic simulations, the HSL team, with the help of a film crew, reenacted clinical cases that mirrored practical scenarios in the ICUs. The video presentations lasted approximately an hour and were presented synchronously to each hospital. As supplementary material, the project delivered educational cards created by the HSL team about assessment scales, vasoactive drugs, and the SAPS-3 severity score.

Beyond the educational cards, the project offered training on filling out the SAPS-3 severity score to encourage the assessment of the severity of hospitalized patients. The training sessions were conducted by the HSL nurses, synchronously lasting a maximum of two hours with all participating ICUs. Participation in the activity was mandatory, requiring at least one professional from each institution.

To promote the continuity of care in the remote ICUs, the project offered online workshops to support the development of clinical protocols and care routines based on up-to-date scientific evidence for implementation in the institutions. The hospitals’ participation was encouraged by the HSL team during the tele-rounds. All remote ICUs that requested support in the activity received it synchronously from the HSL professionals, according to their expertise in the subject.

At the end of the educational activities, except for the online support workshops, participants conducted a satisfaction assessment through the Net Promoter Score (NPS) scale.

### Engagement strategy

During the execution of the project, some engagement strategies were adopted to encourage participation from hospitals and healthcare professionals. These strategies aimed to promote active involvement in tele-rounds and educational activities, adherence to care goals established during tele-rounds, and continuous monitoring of indicators. An adherence goal of over 50% to the clinical decisions established during the multidisciplinary tele-round was set.

To improve engagement, quarterly monitoring meetings were held to critically analyze the indicators and track performance in collaboration with the ICU teams. In addition, a rescue plan was developed, which could be triggered to improve hospital participation and mitigate risks associated with voluntary withdrawals or exclusion from the project. The plan consisted of a monthly evaluation, assigning a score from one to six to the related items. The scores of the evaluated criteria were added up, generating a defined action in the rescue plan (Additional file [1](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#MOESM1)).

### Data analysis

This study employed a mixed-methods analysis to evaluate the implementation process. The qualitative component involved analyzing responses to an open-ended satisfaction question. Statements were categorized as positive, neutral, or negative, and the most frequently mentioned aspects were identified and quantified to synthesize professionals’ perceptions of the implementation workshops.

Regarding the quantitative component, descriptive analyses were conducted to characterize the ICUs and patient population. We also employed descriptive statistics analyses to assess the implementation process and educational activities, using the Metabase platform [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#ref-CR5 "Metabase. 
                  https://www.metabase.com/
                  
                . Accessed 9 Sep 2024.")]. Categorical variables were reported as absolute and relative frequencies, while continuous variables were expressed as means with standard deviations or as medians with interquartile ranges, depending on data distribution.

## Results

The Tele-ICU implementation project lasted for 18 months. Institutions located in 12 cities across four states in Brazil’s North and Northeast regions (Acre, Pará, Maranhão, and Piauí) were followed. During this period, 13 hospitals were assisted, encompassing a total of 15 ICUs and 148 beds, as shown in Table [3](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Tab3). Three other hospitals, which had been previously selected, were excluded during the project (Fig. [2](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Fig2)).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12913-025-12894-4/MediaObjects/12913_2025_12894_Fig2_HTML.png)

Flowchart of selection, replacement, and exclusion of hospitals

During the initial online interview, one hospital with two ICUs was excluded due to a lack of engagement from the medical, physiotherapy, and nursing teams. Later in the project, another hospital with two ICUs was replaced by two new hospitals after participating for less than 10 months, failing to comply with the recommendations outlined in the Adherence and Commitment Agreement. After 12 months, an additional hospital was excluded following the decision of the institution’s new executive management to withdraw from the project. The flowchart detailing hospital selection, replacement, and exclusion is presented in Fig. [2](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Fig2).

Among the 15 ICUs analyzed, 14 were general ICUs that admitted both medical and surgical patients, while one was specialized in trauma and orthopedics. All included ICUs operated as closed units, with attending physicians responsible for managing all patients. None of the participating hospitals had board-certified intensivists on-site.

In the implementation phase of the project, some expected obstacles were encountered. Despite the proposed eight-hour training duration in one day, some hospitals required two days to complete this phase due to internet connection difficulties. Additional barriers included Brazil’s vast geographical dimensions and the logistical difficulties associated with ground and air transportation, which extended the time needed for implementation. In certain locations, the project team remained on-site for up to three days to complete the process.

The implementation workshops involved 141 participants from the remote ICUs. To reduce the inappropriate use of the virtual platform and encourage adherence to the tele-round, participants were trained to instruct other care team members who were not present at this stage. At the end of the implementation workshops, the participants assessed their satisfaction level using the NPS (Net Promoter Score), with an overall average score of 90.8. In addition to scoring their satisfaction, participants described their level of interest in the project using open fields. Most participants provided positive feedback (76 comments; 96.2%), highlighting key aspects such as the positive impact of the project, satisfaction with the implementation workshop, and high levels of participant engagement. Only three comments (3.8%) suggested improvements, specifically regarding the training sessions conducted during implementation. Reports such as “A project that will unify the entire multidisciplinary team in pursuit of a greater goal, which is the well-being of the patient and their family members” or “The possibility of conducting multidisciplinary case discussions between the local team and remote professionals brings immeasurable enrichment to the patient’s treatment, making the project very necessary.” suggest that the teams were engaged and involved in the project. We received many other short encouraging reports such as “very good”, “excellent”, “thank you”, and no negative feedback.

A total of 5,471 unique patients were admitted to the participating ICUs during the project. The mean age was 59.9 years (SD 20.0), and 44.0% were female. The average SAPS-3 score was 52.4 (SD 18.8). On average, more than 70% of admitted patients were evaluated by the HSL team. Factors contributing to the reduced number of patients seen during some tele-rounds included prolonged discussions about more severely ill patients, unstable internet connections, and medical incidents during the tele-round. In patients who were discussed in the tele-rounds, 97% of the daily goals were adhered to by the remote ICU team (Table [3](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Tab3)).

The project conducted 3,971 tele-rounds and 20,168 telehealth visits over the study period. The median duration of telehealth visits was 9 min and 42 s (IQR: 8–11 min), with a proportion of 74.0% of patients receiving at least one telehealth visit per day. Each patient received an average of 2.7 (SD 3.6) telehealth visits during their ICU stay (Table [3](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Tab3)).

The Tele-ICU project offered the possibility of inter-consultation with doctors from specialties not available in the hospitals of the remote ICUs. A total of 142 inter-consultations were carried out, with the majority from the specialty of nutritional medicine (69.7%) and neuro-ICU (23.9%). Other included specialties were dermatology, urology, rheumatology, and ophthalmology.

To support the education of the multidisciplinary teams in the remote ICU and improve the quality of care for critically ill patients, 30 educational activities were conducted, involving 760 health professionals. Of these, 88 (11.6%) were physicians, 340 (44.7%) were registered nurses, 106 (14.0%) were physiotherapists, and 226 (29.7%) were classified as other professionals (Table [4](/articles/10.1186/s12913-025-12894-4#Tab4)). Throughout the project, 17 virtual learn

enriched_points.expanded_content: Mandatory training ensures staff are proficient. Simulating real encounters enhances learning, allowing staff to practice scenarios and build confidence in using tele-ICU technology effectively.

Integrating advanced telemedicine platforms streamlines communication, enhances patient monitoring, and facilitates real-time collaboration among ICU teams, ultimately improving patient outcomes and care efficiency.
Choosing the right operational model, like a hub-and-spoke structure, optimizes resource allocation and extends specialized care to multiple locations, ensuring effective tele-ICU implementation.
Anticipating barriers such as staff resistance and technical issues allows for the development of targeted strategies, ensuring smoother transitions and higher acceptance of tele-ICU initiatives.
